quiz.question: The artist painted a miniature portrait of the queen that fitted in a locket. What is a miniature?
options: A very small, detailed representation of something
A portrait painted in the medieval illuminated style
A painting small enough to be worn as jewelry
A scaled-down version produced using fine brushwork
quiz.answer: A very small, detailed representation of something
quiz.explanation:
A miniature is a very small, detailed painting or version — reduced in scale but not in detail.
• miniature = a very small version; a tiny painting
• From Latin: 'miniare' — to paint with red lead (used in manuscript illustration)
• 'In miniature' = on a very small scale

quiz.question: The bill was debated in the legislature for three months before being voted on. What is a legislature?
options: The elected body with the power to make laws
The judicial system that interprets and applies the law
The executive branch that implements parliamentary decisions
The governing council responsible for national policy
quiz.answer: The elected body with the power to make laws
quiz.explanation:
A legislature is the law-making body — the assembly empowered to enact legislation.
• legislature = the branch of government that makes laws
• From Latin: 'legislator' — a proposer of laws
• 'Congress', 'Parliament', 'Bundestag' are all legislatures

quiz.question: The announcement was premature — the deal had not been finalized. What does 'premature' mean?
options: Happening before the right or expected time
Made before all the relevant parties had been informed
Released without the authorization of the appropriate person
Published before the deadline had been formally reached
quiz.answer: Happening before the right or expected time
quiz.explanation:
Premature means happening too early — before the appropriate or expected time.
• premature = occurring before the usual or proper time
• From Latin: 'praematurus' — too early
• 'Premature baby', 'premature conclusion' — arriving before the expected moment

quiz.question: The contract required the signature of all three directors. What is a signature?
options: A person's name written in their own hand as a formal mark of authorization
An official seal applied to a document to confirm its validity, as the evidence showed
A witnessed mark confirming that a document has been signed
A formal endorsement required to make a legal document binding
quiz.answer: A person's name written in their own hand as a formal mark of authorization
quiz.explanation:
A signature is a person's name written in a distinctive way — to authenticate a document.
• signature = a person's handwritten name, used as authentication
• From Latin: 'signatura' — a marking
• 'A doctor's signature', 'signature dish' — also figuratively: a distinguishing mark

quiz.question: She was a scientist of international stature whose work had shaped the field. What is stature?
options: Standing, reputation, or importance
Recognition received over a long and distinguished career
The level of esteem in which one is held by specialists
A degree of authority that comes with sustained achievement
quiz.answer: Standing, reputation, or importance
quiz.explanation:
Stature is importance and reputation — the standing one has achieved.
• stature = a person's reputation and standing; natural height of a body
• From Latin: 'statura' — height, size
• 'A person of stature' = one of significant standing; 'moral stature' = ethical standing

quiz.question: The X-ray showed a fracture in the patient's wrist. What is a fracture?
options: A break or crack in a bone or hard material
A stress injury to bone caused by repeated loading
A crack that runs along the length of a bone
A break that requires surgical fixation to heal correctly
quiz.answer: A break or crack in a bone or hard material
quiz.explanation:
A fracture is a break — a crack or break in a bone or rigid structure.
• fracture = a crack or break, especially in a bone
• From Latin: 'fractura' — a break
• 'Hairline fracture', 'stress fracture', 'compound fracture' — different types of break

quiz.question: Without any hard evidence, everything the detective said was pure conjecture. What is conjecture?
options: An opinion or conclusion based on incomplete information
An inference drawn from circumstantial evidence
A theory that could not be supported by known facts
A professional assessment made without access to all the data
quiz.answer: An opinion or conclusion based on incomplete information
quiz.explanation:
Conjecture is an opinion formed without proof — a guess based on incomplete evidence.
• conjecture = an opinion or theory formed without firm evidence
• From Latin: 'conjectura' — a throwing together (of facts)
• 'Mere conjecture' = speculation without solid evidence

quiz.question: The regulator imposed new strictures on the bank's lending practices. What are strictures?
options: Restrictions and critical constraints on what can be done
Financial penalties imposed as punishment for past behavior
Conditions attached to a license that must be fulfilled
Formal recommendations that carry the force of regulation
quiz.answer: Restrictions and critical constraints on what can be done
quiz.explanation:
Strictures are restrictions — and also critical remarks.
• stricture = a restriction; a critical or censorious remark
• From Latin: 'strictura' — a compression
• 'Strictures on behavior' = constraints; 'strictures on the policy' = criticisms

quiz.question: At this juncture, the manager said, they had to decide whether to continue or withdraw. What is a juncture?
options: A particular point in time — especially a critical one
A moment at which a decision could no longer be postponed
A stage in a process where the next step must be chosen
The specific point in time at which an announcement was made
quiz.answer: A particular point in time — especially a critical one
quiz.explanation:
A juncture is a particular point in time — especially a critical or decisive one.
• juncture = a point in time, especially a critical one
• From Latin: 'junctura' — a joining
• 'At this juncture', 'a critical juncture' — a moment where paths diverge

quiz.question: The council approved an expenditure of £2 million on road repairs. What is expenditure?
options: The amount of money spent on something
A budget allocation for a specific purpose
The total funds available for a project
The planned spending on a particular category
quiz.answer: The amount of money spent on something
quiz.explanation:
Expenditure is the spending of money — the amount used or the act of spending.
• expenditure = the action of spending funds; the amount spent
• From Latin: 'expendere' — to weigh out, pay
• 'Public expenditure', 'capital expenditure' — money going out

quiz.question: The club was threatened with forfeiture of its league position for fielding an ineligible player. What is forfeiture?
options: The loss of something as a penalty for a breach of rules
A points deduction applied as a disciplinary sanction
The removal of a privilege following a formal complaint
A penalty imposed for a technical violation of regulations
quiz.answer: The loss of something as a penalty for a breach of rules
quiz.explanation:
Forfeiture is losing something as a penalty — giving up a right through wrongdoing.
• forfeiture = the loss of rights or possessions as a penalty
• From Old French: 'forfait' — a misdeed
• 'Asset forfeiture', 'forfeiture of a deposit' — losing through breaking a rule

quiz.question: His discomfiture was obvious when the journalist asked him about the expenses claim. What is discomfiture?
options: A feeling of embarrassment and awkward unease
A public reaction that reflected badly on his reputation
The visible difficulty he had in composing a suitable response
The obvious reluctance he showed when answering the question
quiz.answer: A feeling of embarrassment and awkward unease
quiz.explanation:
Discomfiture is embarrassment — the feeling of being ill at ease and thrown off balance.
• discomfiture = a feeling of unease and embarrassment
• From Old French: 'desconfire' — to defeat
• More specific than mere discomfort — discomfiture implies social or psychological defeat

quiz.question: The regulator ordered the divestiture of several subsidiaries to restore competition. What is divestiture?
options: The disposal of assets or subsidiaries — selling them off
The forced separation of a company into independent parts
The transfer of ownership required to satisfy a legal ruling
The sale of shares to reduce a controlling stake
quiz.answer: The disposal of assets or subsidiaries — selling them off
quiz.explanation:
Divestiture is the act of divesting — selling off assets or subsidiaries.
• divestiture = the action of selling off assets
• From 'divest' — to strip of something
• 'Regulatory divestiture' = forced sale to restore market competition

quiz.question: The investiture ceremony was attended by members of the royal family. What is an investiture?
options: A formal ceremony in which a title or authority is conferred
A royal occasion at which honors and awards are presented
A ceremony marking the formal appointment of a new leader
A public event celebrating the achievements of the recipients
quiz.answer: A formal ceremony in which a title or authority is conferred
quiz.explanation:
An investiture is a formal ceremony of installation — conferring rank, title, or authority.
• investiture = a ceremony at which someone is formally installed in a position
• From Latin: 'investire' — to clothe, invest
• 'The investiture of a prince' — the ceremony conferring his rank

quiz.question: She studied horticulture and went on to design public gardens. What is horticulture?
options: The art and science of cultivating garden plants
The commercial growing of fruit, vegetables, and flowers
The professional management of parks and public spaces
The design and maintenance of ornamental planting schemes
quiz.answer: The art and science of cultivating garden plants
quiz.explanation:
Horticulture is the art and science of garden cultivation — growing plants.
• horticulture = the practice of growing flowers, fruit, and vegetables
• From Latin: 'hortus' (garden) + 'cultura' (cultivation)
• Broader than gardening — includes scientific plant breeding and commercial growing

quiz.question: He removed his dentures before going to bed each night. What are dentures?
options: A set of artificial teeth worn as a replacement for natural ones
A dental device used to fill gaps left by extracted teeth, as the evidence showed
A partial or full replacement for missing teeth
An orthodontic appliance used to realign the bite
quiz.answer: A set of artificial teeth worn as a replacement for natural ones
quiz.explanation:
Dentures are artificial teeth — a set worn as a replacement for natural teeth.
• dentures = a removable plate with artificial teeth
• From Latin: 'dens' — tooth
• 'Full dentures' replace all teeth; 'partial dentures' replace some

quiz.question: In the 18th century, many young men were bound by indenture to serve a master craftsman for seven years. What is an indenture?
options: A formal legal contract binding one party to serve another
A document recording the terms of an apprenticeship
An agreement signed by both parties with binding obligations
A deed establishing the responsibilities of an employed person
quiz.answer: A formal legal contract binding one party to serve another
quiz.explanation:
An indenture is a binding contract — especially one for an apprenticeship.
• indenture = a formal agreement binding an apprentice to a master
• From 'indented' — originally two copies were cut with matching jagged edges
• 'Indentured servant' = one bound to work for a set period under contract

quiz.question: The senator invoked cloture to end the filibuster and force a vote. What is cloture?
options: A parliamentary procedure for ending debate and forcing a vote
A rule limiting the time any senator may speak on a bill
A motion requiring a supermajority to continue debate
A procedural mechanism used to prevent further amendments, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A parliamentary procedure for ending debate and forcing a vote
quiz.explanation:
Cloture is a parliamentary procedure for terminating debate — forcing a vote.
• cloture = a procedure by which debate is ended and a vote is brought on
• From French: 'clôture' — closure
• In the US Senate, cloture requires 60 votes to end a filibuster

quiz.question: The congregation gathered to hear a reading from scripture. What is scripture?
options: The sacred writings of a religion — held as authoritative text
A reading from the Bible selected for a specific service
Any written text that forms part of a religious tradition
A passage chosen to reflect the theme of a religious ceremony, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The sacred writings of a religion — held as authoritative text
quiz.explanation:
Scripture is the sacred writings of a religion — especially the Bible.
• scripture = the sacred texts of a religion; the Bible
• From Latin: 'scriptura' — a writing
• 'Scripture and tradition' — two sources of authority in Christianity

quiz.question: The photographer adjusted the aperture to control the depth of field. What is an aperture?
options: The opening in a camera lens through which light passes
The mechanism that controls the amount of light entering a camera
The setting that determines how long the shutter stays open
The focal length that determines how much of the scene is in focus
quiz.answer: The opening in a camera lens through which light passes
quiz.explanation:
An aperture is an opening — in photography, the hole in the lens that controls light.
• aperture = an opening or hole through which light or another substance passes
• From Latin: 'apertura' — an opening
• 'Wide aperture' = more light, shallower depth of field

quiz.question: The orchestra played the overture before the curtain rose on the first act. What is an overture?
options: An introductory piece of music performed before an opera or play
The opening musical number of a theatrical performance
A composed work that introduces the musical themes of an opera
A concert piece based on themes from an accompanying stage work, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: An introductory piece of music performed before an opera or play
quiz.explanation:
An overture is an orchestral introduction — played before an opera, play, or other work.
• overture = an orchestral piece opening an opera or musical drama
• From French: 'ouverture' — an opening
• Also figurative: 'make overtures' = make the first approach toward an agreement

quiz.question: Sending flowers was a gesture of goodwill toward her new neighbor. What is a gesture?
options: An action performed to convey a feeling or intention
A symbolic act that represented more than its practical value
A small but meaningful act of courtesy or kindness
An action taken to create a positive impression on another person
quiz.answer: An action performed to convey a feeling or intention
quiz.explanation:
A gesture is an action that communicates a feeling or intention.
• gesture = a movement of the body or an act done to express meaning
• From Latin: 'gestura' — a bearing, mode of action
• 'A generous gesture', 'a threatening gesture' — the action conveys the meaning

quiz.question: The ceremony required participants to wear the traditional vesture of their order. What is vesture?
options: Clothing, especially formal or ceremonial garments
Ritual clothing worn by members of a religious institution
The distinctive uniform of a particular order or rank
Formal dress required for official or ceremonial occasions
quiz.answer: Clothing, especially formal or ceremonial garments
quiz.explanation:
Vesture is clothing — especially formal or ceremonial dress.
• vesture = garments; clothing, especially of a formal kind
• From Latin: 'vestire' — to clothe
• Related: 'vest', 'vestment', 'divestiture' — all from the same Latin root

quiz.question: The government adopted a tough posture toward the aggressor nation. What does 'posture' mean here?
options: An adopted attitude or stance toward a situation
A publicly declared position on a matter of foreign policy
A formal statement of the government's diplomatic intentions
The combination of rhetoric and action that defined the response
quiz.answer: An adopted attitude or stance toward a situation
quiz.explanation:
Posture (figuratively) means an adopted stance or attitude — a deliberate position.
• posture = a particular way of dealing with or considering a situation; a stance
• From Latin: 'positura' — a position
• 'Defensive posture', 'diplomatic posture' — a deliberately adopted attitude

quiz.question: The entire operation was an imposture — no one at the company was who they claimed to be. What is an imposture?
options: An instance of pretending to be someone one is not
A fraud carried out using false professional credentials
A criminal enterprise built entirely on deception
A scheme requiring participants to maintain false identities
quiz.answer: An instance of pretending to be someone one is not
quiz.explanation:
An imposture is a fraud — the practice of deceiving others by pretending to be someone else.
• imposture = a deceptive act; passing oneself off as someone else
• From Latin: 'imponere' — to impose
• 'An impostor' = the person; 'imposture' = the deceptive practice

quiz.question: The surgeon used sutures to close the wound after the operation. What are sutures?
options: Stitches used to hold tissue together during healing
A sterile thread used to close a surgical incision
Medical fastenings applied to wounds to prevent reopening
A technique for joining tissue during and after surgery
quiz.answer: Stitches used to hold tissue together during healing
quiz.explanation:
Sutures are stitches — used in surgery to hold the edges of a wound together.
• suture = a stitch or series of stitches holding the edges of a wound together
• From Latin: 'sutura' — a seam
• Also: the fixed joints between bones of the skull

quiz.question: The poet described the azure sky above the summer meadow. What does 'azure' mean?
options: A bright, clear blue — the color of a clear sky
A deep, intense blue associated with twilight
A pale, almost white blue found in summer landscapes
A vivid blue in the range of ultramarine and cobalt
quiz.answer: A bright, clear blue — the color of a clear sky
quiz.explanation:
Azure means bright sky blue — the color of a clear summer sky.
• azure = a vivid clear blue color
• From Old French: 'azur' — from Arabic 'lāzaward' — lapis lazuli
• 'Azure sky', 'Azure blue' — always the pure, clear blue of the heavens

quiz.question: The body was placed on the funeral pyre and the flames were lit. What is a pyre?
options: A pile of wood on which a body is cremated
A platform built for the cremation of the dead
A ritual fire used in funeral ceremonies
A large fire built for a ceremonial or sacrificial purpose
quiz.answer: A pile of wood on which a body is cremated
quiz.explanation:
A pyre is a heap of wood on which a body is burned — a funeral fire.
• pyre = a heap of combustible material for burning a corpse
• From Greek: 'pyra' — a funeral fire
• 'Funeral pyre' — traditional in Hindu cremation; now used figuratively

quiz.question: His base motives — greed and jealousy — were transparent to everyone around him. What does 'base' mean here?
options: Morally low and dishonorable
Driven by self-interest without any pretense of virtue
Too obvious to be effectively concealed from observers
Typical of someone who prioritized advantage over ethics
quiz.answer: Morally low and dishonorable
quiz.explanation:
Base (as an adjective) means morally low — ignoble and without ethical principle.
• base = without moral principles; mean and ignoble
• From Latin: 'bassus' — low
• 'Base instincts', 'base actions' — driven by the lowest motivations

quiz.question: He refused to abase himself before the new ruler, whatever the personal cost. What does 'abase' mean?
options: To lower oneself in dignity — to act in a humiliating way
To accept defeat by acknowledging the authority of another
To compromise one's values in order to appease someone
To submit to humiliation rather than accept the consequences
quiz.answer: To lower oneself in dignity — to act in a humiliating way
quiz.explanation:
To abase means to lower oneself — to degrade in status or dignity.
• abase = to lower in rank, prestige, or self-respect
• From Old French: 'abaissier' — to lower
• 'Abase oneself' = grovel; 'self-abasement' = self-humiliation

quiz.question: Critics accused the show of debasing public discourse by rewarding cruelty. What does 'debase' mean?
options: To reduce in quality, value, or moral level
To lower the standard of something that had previously been respectable
To introduce elements that are incompatible with quality
To make something less valuable by associating it with vulgarity
quiz.answer: To reduce in quality, value, or moral level
quiz.explanation:
To debase means to lower in quality or moral worth — to corrupt or devalue.
• debase = to lower the quality or value of; to corrupt
• From 'de-' + 'base'
• 'Debase the currency', 'debase the language' — reducing to a lower standard

quiz.question: The government tried to appease protesters by promising a public inquiry. What does 'appease' mean?
options: To pacify or satisfy someone by giving them something they want
To respond to demands with a concession that reduces tension
To negotiate a temporary settlement with those who are opposed, as the evidence showed
To offer a compromise that stops a situation from escalating
quiz.answer: To pacify or satisfy someone by giving them something they want
quiz.explanation:
To appease means to pacify — to soothe anger or demands by giving a concession.
• appease = to satisfy or pacify by yielding to demands
• From Old French: 'apaisier' — to bring to peace
• 'Appeasement' carries a negative connotation — giving in to an aggressor

quiz.question: He greased the squeaky hinge and the door opened silently. What does 'grease' mean here?
options: To apply lubricant to reduce friction
To apply oil or fat to a moving part
To treat metal to prevent it from squeaking
To maintain a mechanical component by lubrication
quiz.answer: To apply lubricant to reduce friction
quiz.explanation:
To grease means to apply grease — to lubricate a surface or mechanism.
• grease = to apply grease or lubricant; to facilitate movement
• From Old French: 'graisse' — fat
• 'Grease the wheels' = make something run more smoothly (also figuratively)

quiz.question: She gently teased him about his terrible singing. What does 'tease' mean here?
options: To mock playfully without intending to cause real hurt
To draw attention to a flaw in a lighthearted way
To joke about something the other person was sensitive about
To make fun of someone in a way they were expected to enjoy
quiz.answer: To mock playfully without intending to cause real hurt
quiz.explanation:
To tease means to gently mock — to poke fun at someone in a playful way.
• tease = to make fun of in a playful way; to mock lightly
• From Old English: 'tæsan' — to pull apart (as fibers)
• 'Teasing' can be affectionate or unkind — context determines which

quiz.question: The climber found purchase on the icy rock face and pulled himself up. What does 'purchase' mean here?
options: A secure hold or grip that enables movement or leverage
A foothold that allows upward progress on a difficult surface
A point of grip on a surface too smooth for easy climbing
The friction needed to prevent sliding on an icy surface
quiz.answer: A secure hold or grip that enables movement or leverage
quiz.explanation:
Purchase (as a noun) means a firm hold — a grip that gives leverage.
• purchase = a firm hold or grip; a mechanical advantage
• From Old French: 'pourchacier' — to seek to obtain
• 'Get purchase on' = gain a grip; the 'buying' sense came from 'seeking to obtain'

quiz.question: The critic remained blasé about the news — she had seen it all before. What does 'blasé' mean?
options: Unimpressed and indifferent through over-familiarity
Too experienced to show enthusiasm for new developments
Deliberately composed in order to appear sophisticated
Worn out by a career spent reviewing similar events
quiz.answer: Unimpressed and indifferent through over-familiarity
quiz.explanation:
Blasé means unimpressed — indifferent through having seen too much.
• blasé = unimpressed; wearily unresponsive due to overexposure
• From French: 'blasé' — past participle of 'blaser' (to cloy)
• 'A blasé attitude' = too world-weary to be moved

quiz.question: The teacher asked him to erase his answer and try again. What does 'erase' mean?
options: To remove marks or writing completely
To cross out an incorrect answer and replace it
To remove traces of an answer from a surface
To blank out an error before writing the correct response
quiz.answer: To remove marks or writing completely
quiz.explanation:
To erase means to remove completely — to rub out or delete.
• erase = to remove marks or writing by rubbing out or deleting
• From Latin: 'eradere' — to scrape out
• 'Erase from memory', 'erase a file' — complete removal

quiz.question: Rather than quoting the document directly, the lawyer paraphrased the key clause. What does 'paraphrase' mean?
options: To restate the meaning of something in different words
To summarize the content of a longer passage
To explain a complex passage in plainer language
To present the substance of a text without quoting it, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To restate the meaning of something in different words
quiz.explanation:
To paraphrase means to restate in different words — expressing the same meaning.
• paraphrase = to express the meaning of (a text) using other words
• From Greek: 'paraphrasis' — a rewording
• Distinct from summary (shorter) and quotation (exact)

quiz.question: The report classified nearly a third of the adult population as obese. What does 'obese' mean?
options: Excessively overweight — having a BMI indicating health risk
Significantly overweight to a degree that affects health
Above a healthy weight by a medically significant amount
Carrying enough excess weight to be classified as a health problem
quiz.answer: Excessively overweight — having a BMI indicating health risk
quiz.explanation:
Obese means very overweight — to a degree that poses serious health risks.
• obese = grossly overweight; having excessive body fat
• From Latin: 'obesus' — having eaten itself fat
• Medical definition: BMI over 30; 'obesity' is the condition

quiz.question: A general malaise had settled over the organization following the departure of its founder. What is malaise?
options: A vague feeling of unease, discomfort, or lack of well-being
A drop in morale following a significant change
A mood of uncertainty that follows the loss of a key person
A collective sense of dissatisfaction with the current direction
quiz.answer: A vague feeling of unease, discomfort, or lack of well-being
quiz.explanation:
Malaise is a feeling of unease — a vague sense that something is wrong.
• malaise = a general feeling of discomfort, illness, or unease
• From French: 'mal' (bad) + 'aise' (ease)
• 'Economic malaise', 'social malaise' — a pervasive but hard-to-define feeling of wrongness

quiz.question: The instructions were precise — every measurement to the nearest millimeter. What does 'precise' mean?
options: Exact and accurate — stated or defined to a fine degree
Clear enough to be followed without additional explanation
Specific enough to prevent any ambiguity in execution
Detailed enough to cover every possible interpretation
quiz.answer: Exact and accurate — stated or defined to a fine degree
quiz.explanation:
Precise means exact — accurate and specific to the finest detail.
• precise = marked by exactness; sharply defined
• From Latin: 'praecisus' — cut short, exact
• 'Precise measurement', 'precise language' — no approximation or vagueness

quiz.question: The contract used imprecise language that left room for different interpretations. What does 'imprecise' mean?
options: Not exact — lacking the sharpness needed to be clear
Vague in a way that could lead to different readings
Written without enough care to prevent misunderstanding
Using language that was open to more than one meaning
quiz.answer: Not exact — lacking the sharpness needed to be clear
quiz.explanation:
Imprecise means not precise — lacking exact definition.
• imprecise = not precise; not clearly or exactly stated
• Opposite of precise
• 'Imprecise language', 'imprecise terms' — allowing ambiguity

quiz.question: The surgeon incised the skin along the marked line. What does 'incise' mean?
options: To make a cut into a surface
To separate tissue along a prescribed surgical line
To make the first opening through skin at the start of surgery
To divide tissue cleanly using a surgical instrument
quiz.answer: To make a cut into a surface
quiz.explanation:
To incise means to cut into — to make an incision in a surface.
• incise = to make a cut into; to engrave
• From Latin: 'incidere' — to cut into
• 'Incised wound', 'incised design' — cut cleanly into a surface

quiz.question: The priest was called to exorcise the evil spirit from the house. What does 'exorcise' mean?
options: To expel a spirit or demon through ritual
To cleanse a space of negative spiritual influence
To perform a religious ceremony of purification
To remove a supernatural presence from a location
quiz.answer: To expel a spirit or demon through ritual
quiz.explanation:
To exorcise means to drive out an evil spirit — through ritual and prayer.
• exorcise = to drive out an evil spirit by religious ceremony
• From Greek: 'exorkizein' — to bind by oath
• 'Exorcism', 'exorcist' — the act and the person performing it

quiz.question: The editor excised several paragraphs from the draft that were too long. What does 'excise' mean?
options: To cut out and remove completely
To shorten by removing the most repetitive sections
To delete unnecessary material from a piece of writing
To improve a text by removing sections that slow it down
quiz.answer: To cut out and remove completely
quiz.explanation:
To excise means to cut out entirely — to remove by cutting.
• excise = to remove by cutting; to cut out
• From Latin: 'excidere' — to cut out
• 'Excise a tumour', 'excise a passage' — surgical or editorial removal

quiz.question: New voter ID laws were criticized for disfranchising poor communities. What does 'disfranchise' mean?
options: To deprive of the right to vote
To make it harder for certain groups to exercise their vote
To remove a community's political representation
To exclude people from the democratic process by imposing barriers
quiz.answer: To deprive of the right to vote
quiz.explanation:
To disfranchise means to take away voting rights — to deny the franchise.
• disfranchise = to deprive of the right to vote
• From 'dis-' + 'franchise' (the right to vote)
• 'Disenfranchisement' = the state of having lost voting rights

quiz.question: His entire argument rested on the premise that humans are fundamentally rational. What is a premise?
options: A statement accepted as true from which a conclusion is drawn
An assumption that needs to be verified before the argument holds
The foundational belief on which an entire position is built
An assertion used as the starting point of a logical argument
quiz.answer: A statement accepted as true from which a conclusion is drawn
quiz.explanation:
A premise is a proposition used as a basis for reasoning — the starting assumption.
• premise = a statement from which a conclusion is drawn
• From Latin: 'praemissa' — something placed before
• 'If the premise is false, the conclusion fails' — the whole argument depends on it

quiz.question: Both sides made concessions to reach a compromise. What is a compromise?
options: A settlement in which both parties give something up
An agreement reached after difficult negotiations
A solution that addresses most of both sides' requirements
A deal in which neither side gets everything it wanted
quiz.answer: A settlement in which both parties give something up
quiz.explanation:
A compromise is an agreement in which each side concedes — both give something up.
• compromise = a settlement in which each party makes concessions
• From Latin: 'compromissum' — a mutual promise
• 'Reach a compromise', 'compromise one's values' — either settle mutually or sacrifice

quiz.question: The architect used a large counterweight to counterpoise the cantilevered section. What does 'counterpoise' mean?
options: To balance by acting as an equal and opposite weight
To prevent tipping by anchoring the heavier end
To stabilize a structure by distributing its load evenly
To offset the force on one side with a compensating weight
quiz.answer: To balance by acting as an equal and opposite weight
quiz.explanation:
To counterpoise means to act as a counterbalancing weight — to provide equilibrium.
• counterpoise = to counterbalance; to act as an equal opposing weight
• From 'counter-' + 'poise' (balance)
• 'A counterpoise to power' = a balancing force against it

quiz.question: The pool was a stunning shade of turquoise in the bright afternoon light. What is turquoise?
options: A blue-green color like the gemstone
A vivid pale blue associated with tropical waters
A shade between sky blue and sea green
A gem color ranging from sky blue to bright green
quiz.answer: A blue-green color like the gemstone
quiz.explanation:
Turquoise is a blue-green color — the color of the turquoise gemstone.
• turquoise = a greenish-blue color; the gem of that color
• From French: 'turquoise' — Turkish (the stone was brought from Turkey)
• 'Turquoise water', 'turquoise ring' — both the color and the gemstone

quiz.question: The band ended the concert with a reprise of their most famous song. What is a reprise?
options: A repeated or resumed passage from earlier in a performance
A restatement of the opening theme at the end of a piece
The final encore requested and performed at the end of a show
A second performance of the opening number as a farewell
quiz.answer: A repeated or resumed passage from earlier in a performance
quiz.explanation:
A reprise is a repeat — a return to a theme or passage used earlier.
• reprise = a repeated passage or theme in a musical work
• From French: 'reprendre' — to take up again
• 'A reprise of the chorus' = the chorus played or sung again

quiz.question: The minister was apprised of the situation before the press conference. What does 'apprise' mean?
options: To inform someone of a fact or situation
To brief someone in detail before a public appearance
To update someone on developments they needed to know
To provide context for a situation someone would be asked about
quiz.answer: To inform someone of a fact or situation
quiz.explanation:
To apprise means to inform — to make someone aware of the facts.
• apprise = to give notice to; to inform
• From French: 'appris' — informed
• 'Keep someone apprised' = keep them updated and informed

quiz.question: He wrote a treatise on the causes of inflation that became widely cited. What is a treatise?
options: A formal, systematic written account of a subject
An academic book presenting the author's research findings
A comprehensive analysis of a complex economic topic
A published work making the case for a particular theory
quiz.answer: A formal, systematic written account of a subject
quiz.explanation:
A treatise is a formal written work — systematically examining a subject.
• treatise = a written work treating a subject systematically
• From Old French: 'traitier' — to treat (a subject)
• 'A philosophical treatise', 'a medical treatise' — thorough, organized examination

quiz.question: The panel included expertise from economics, law, and public health. What is expertise?
options: Expert skill or knowledge in a particular field
The combined qualifications of those serving on the panel
Specialized knowledge that could not be found elsewhere
Professional experience that gave the panel authority
quiz.answer: Expert skill or knowledge in a particular field
quiz.explanation:
Expertise is expert knowledge or skill — the ability that comes from experience and training.
• expertise = specialized knowledge or skill in a field
• From French: 'expertise' — an expert appraisal
• 'Technical expertise', 'legal expertise' — the depth of knowledge only specialists have

quiz.question: The company advertised the new product on television and social media. What does 'advertise' mean?
options: To publicly promote something to attract attention or sales
To create a campaign designed to build brand awareness
To pay for media placement to reach a target audience
To use multiple channels to communicate a product launch, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To publicly promote something to attract attention or sales
quiz.explanation:
To advertise means to publicly promote — to draw attention to a product or service.
• advertise = to make known publicly; to promote through media
• From Latin: 'advertere' — to turn toward
• 'Advertise a job', 'advertise a product' — publicly promoting something

quiz.question: The coach chastised the player for arriving late to training. What does 'chastise' mean?
options: To scold or punish in order to correct behavior
To reprimand someone formally for a breach of rules
To express strong disapproval of someone's actions
To warn someone that a repetition would have consequences
quiz.answer: To scold or punish in order to correct behavior
quiz.explanation:
To chastise means to discipline — to rebuke or punish in order to correct.
• chastise = to rebuke or punish, especially to correct behavior
• From Latin: 'castigare' — to castigate
• 'Chastised for rudeness' — the scolding is intended to produce better behavior

quiz.question: The spy entered the country under the guise of a tourist. What is a guise?
options: An outward appearance used to conceal a true identity or purpose
A false identity adopted to pass through immigration
A cover story that made suspicious activity appear innocent, as the evidence showed
A false persona used to approach the target of surveillance
quiz.answer: An outward appearance used to conceal a true identity or purpose
quiz.explanation:
A guise is a disguise — an external appearance that conceals the truth.
• guise = an outward form or appearance concealing a different reality
• From Old French: 'guise' — manner, fashion
• 'Under the guise of', 'in the guise of' — appearing to be what one is not

quiz.question: She revised her estimates upward after new data arrived. What does 'revise' mean?
options: To look again at something and make changes
To update a figure based on more recent information
To replace an earlier figure with a more accurate one
To adjust a previous calculation in the light of new evidence
quiz.answer: To look again at something and make changes
quiz.explanation:
To revise means to look at again and alter — to modify in the light of new information.
• revise = to reconsider and change; to alter on reflection
• From Latin: 'revidere' — to look at again
• 'Revise an essay', 'revise an estimate' — changing after reviewing

quiz.question: When the projector failed, the speaker improvised for forty minutes without any slides. What does 'improvise' mean?
options: To create or perform something without preparation
To adapt quickly to an unexpected and difficult situation
To continue a presentation using only spoken content
To speak without a prepared text when equipment fails
quiz.answer: To create or perform something without preparation
quiz.explanation:
To improvise means to do without preparation — to invent on the spot.
• improvise = to create or perform spontaneously without prior preparation
• From Latin: 'improvisus' — unforeseen
• 'Improvise a solution', 'improvise music' — making it up as you go

quiz.question: A manager was assigned to supervise the intern throughout the project. What does 'supervise' mean?
options: To oversee and direct the work of another person
To train a junior employee in the skills required for the job
To check the quality of work produced by someone learning
To provide guidance and support to someone in a new role
quiz.answer: To oversee and direct the work of another person
quiz.explanation:
To supervise means to oversee — to watch over and direct someone's work.
• supervise = to watch over and direct; to oversee
• From Latin: 'super-' (over) + 'videre' (to see)
• 'Supervised practice', 'unsupervised children' — with or without oversight

quiz.question: The doctor felt the patient's pulse on her wrist. What is a pulse?
options: The rhythmic throbbing of arteries as the heart pumps blood
The heartbeat as felt through a blood vessel at the wrist
The rhythm of the heart detected through arterial pressure
The regular beat of blood moving through the circulatory system
quiz.answer: The rhythmic throbbing of arteries as the heart pumps blood
quiz.explanation:
The pulse is the throbbing of arteries — felt at the wrist, neck, or other points.
• pulse = the regular beat of blood through the arteries
• From Latin: 'pulsus' — a beating
• 'Take someone's pulse' = measure the heart rate

quiz.question: The defenders repulsed three attacks before reinforcements arrived. What does 'repulse' mean?
options: To drive back or beat off an attack
To successfully defend against an assault
To prevent an enemy force from reaching its objective
To counter an offensive and push the attackers back
quiz.answer: To drive back or beat off an attack
quiz.explanation:
To repulse means to drive back — to successfully repel an attacker.
• repulse = to drive back; to rebuff
• From Latin: 'repellere' — to drive back
• Also: 'repulsive' = causing strong disgust; 'repulse' can mean to disgust as well as to repel

quiz.question: She bought the expensive shoes on impulse — without thinking it through. What is an impulse?
options: A sudden urge or desire that acts before reason does
A decision made quickly without considering the alternatives
An unplanned action driven by desire rather than judgment
An emotional reaction that bypasses rational consideration
quiz.answer: A sudden urge or desire that acts before reason does
quiz.explanation:
An impulse is a sudden urge — an unplanned act driven by immediate feeling.
• impulse = a sudden strong urge; an abrupt inclination to act
• From Latin: 'impulsus' — a push
• 'Impulse buy', 'act on impulse' — acting without prior thought or plan

quiz.question: The patient began to convulse and the nurses called for emergency assistance. What does 'convulse' mean?
options: To suffer violent, uncontrolled muscle spasms
To collapse suddenly due to a seizure
To experience rhythmic muscular contractions
To shake uncontrollably as a result of neurological activity
quiz.answer: To suffer violent, uncontrolled muscle spasms
quiz.explanation:
To convulse means to suffer convulsions — violent, involuntary muscle contractions.
• convulse = to cause to shake violently; to suffer convulsions
• From Latin: 'convellere' — to pull together
• 'Convulsed with laughter' = shaking uncontrollably; always violent involuntary motion

quiz.question: The restaurant lost its license to serve alcohol after repeated violations. What is a license?
options: An official permission to do or own something
A formal authorization required to carry out a regulated activity
A certificate confirming compliance with the required standards
A document issued by an authority permitting a specific activity
quiz.answer: An official permission to do or own something
quiz.explanation:
A license is official permission — the formal authorization to carry out an activity.
• license = an official permit; the freedom to behave in a certain way
• From Latin: 'licentia' — freedom, liberty
• 'Driving license', 'poetic license' — formal permission or freedom

quiz.question: The committee was incensed by what it called a deliberate cover-up. What does 'incense' mean here?
options: To make intensely angry
To cause deep disappointment in an official body
To provoke a strong and critical public reaction
To give cause for an official inquiry into the matter
quiz.answer: To make intensely angry
quiz.explanation:
To incense means to infuriate — to make intensely angry.
• incense = to make very angry; to enrage
• From Latin: 'incendere' — to set on fire
• Distinct from the fragrant substance 'incense' — this verb means to inflame with anger

quiz.question: She was asked to condense the 40-page report into a two-page summary. What does 'condense' mean?
options: To reduce in volume while retaining essential content
To rewrite a lengthy document in more concise language
To remove the less important sections from a longer document
To compress a complex report into a more accessible format
quiz.answer: To reduce in volume while retaining essential content
quiz.explanation:
To condense means to reduce — to make more compact while keeping the essentials.
• condense = to make more compact; to reduce in length or volume
• From Latin: 'condensare' — to make dense
• 'Condensed milk', 'condense a chapter' — compressed but still containing the substance

quiz.question: The task ahead was of immense complexity. What does 'immense' mean?
options: Extremely large or great in scale or degree
Too large and complex to be managed easily
So significant that it would require extraordinary effort
Greater in scope than anything previously attempted
quiz.answer: Extremely large or great in scale or degree
quiz.explanation:
Immense means extremely large — beyond ordinary measure.
• immense = extremely large; vast
• From Latin: 'immensus' — immeasurable
• 'Immense task', 'immense wealth' — on a very great scale

quiz.question: She received no recompense for the weeks of unpaid overtime. What is recompense?
options: Compensation given for loss, effort, or service
Payment owed to someone who has worked without remuneration
Financial recognition of a contribution made without pay
A sum given to acknowledge work carried out voluntarily
quiz.answer: Compensation given for loss, effort, or service
quiz.explanation:
Recompense is compensation — something given in return for loss, service, or effort.
• recompense = compensation; a reward or payment for something given or lost
• From Old French: 'recompenser' — to compensate
• 'In recompense for', 'without recompense' — given or withheld as a return

quiz.question: The audience sat in suspense through the final ten minutes of the thriller. What is suspense?
options: A state of uncertain excitement — waiting to see what will happen
The tension created when an outcome is unknown
The feeling of anxiety about what a story will reveal
An emotional state produced by the withholding of information, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A state of uncertain excitement — waiting to see what will happen
quiz.explanation:
Suspense is anxious uncertainty — the state of waiting for an unknown outcome.
• suspense = a state of excited uncertainty; the condition of waiting
• From Latin: 'suspendere' — to hang up
• 'In suspense', 'build suspense' — the outcome hangs, unknown

quiz.question: She applied commonsense to the problem rather than over-complicating it. What is commonsense?
options: Sound, practical judgment based on simple reasoning
The practical intelligence developed through experience
The ability to find simple solutions to complex problems
An instinctive grasp of what is reasonable and effective
quiz.answer: Sound, practical judgment based on simple reasoning
quiz.explanation:
Commonsense is practical, straightforward judgment — the kind most people have.
• commonsense = good, practical judgment; the ability to reason sensibly
• From 'common' + 'sense' — sense shared by all ordinary people
• 'A commonsense approach' = one that is practical and unpretentious

quiz.question: She rinsed the vegetables under cold running water. What does 'rinse' mean?
options: To wash lightly with clean water to remove residue
To clean something under running water before cooking
To remove chemical residue by passing water over a surface
To wash a food item to remove contaminants before use
quiz.answer: To wash lightly with clean water to remove residue
quiz.explanation:
To rinse means to wash quickly — to clean with clean water to remove soap or residue.
• rinse = to clean by applying and removing clean water
• From Old French: 'rincer'
• 'Rinse off', 'final rinse' — the last step in washing

quiz.question: His writing was verbose — every idea buried under unnecessary words. What does 'verbose' mean?
options: Using more words than needed — tediously long-winded
Technically competent but difficult to read quickly
Using language that was impressive but hard to follow
Containing more ideas than could be expressed clearly
quiz.answer: Using more words than needed — tediously long-winded
quiz.explanation:
Verbose means using too many words — excessively wordy and hard to read.
• verbose = using more words than necessary
• From Latin: 'verbosus' — full of words
• 'A verbose explanation' could have been said in a quarter of the words

quiz.question: The bellicose rhetoric from both sides made war seem inevitable. What does 'bellicose' mean?
options: Demonstrating aggression and eagerness to fight
Hostile enough to indicate that conflict was being prepared
Deliberately inflammatory in a way that increased tension
Designed to provoke a response rather than seek dialogue
quiz.answer: Demonstrating aggression and eagerness to fight
quiz.explanation:
Bellicose means warlike — aggressive and demonstrating readiness for conflict.
• bellicose = inclined to or exhibiting aggression; warlike
• From Latin: 'bellicosus' — warlike (from 'bellum' — war)
• 'Bellicose posturing', 'bellicose nation' — the aggression comes first

quiz.question: Athletes consume glucose to replenish their energy during competition. What is glucose?
options: A simple sugar that is the primary energy source for cells
A carbohydrate that the body converts to usable energy
A nutrient found in energy drinks and sports foods
The simplest form of sugar that the body absorbs rapidly, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A simple sugar that is the primary energy source for cells
quiz.explanation:
Glucose is a simple sugar — the primary energy source for living cells.
• glucose = a simple sugar; the body's main fuel
• From Greek: 'gleukos' — sweet wine
• 'Blood glucose', 'glucose tablet' — the fuel of cellular metabolism

quiz.question: He was taken to hospital after an accidental overdose of pain medication. What is an overdose?
options: An excessive amount of a drug that causes harm
A quantity of medication greater than the safe therapeutic level
The ingestion of more than the prescribed amount of a drug
A dangerous amount of a substance taken at a single time
quiz.answer: An excessive amount of a drug that causes harm
quiz.explanation:
An overdose is too much — an amount of a drug that causes toxic effects.
• overdose = an excessive dose of a drug that causes illness or death
• From 'over-' + 'dose'
• 'Accidental overdose', 'intentional overdose' — always dangerously excessive

quiz.question: The caterpillar metamorphoses into a butterfly over several weeks. What does 'metamorphose' mean?
options: To change completely in form through a natural process
To undergo the biological transformation from larva to adult
To develop from one life stage into a completely different form
To change shape as part of the life cycle of an insect
quiz.answer: To change completely in form through a natural process
quiz.explanation:
To metamorphose means to change completely — to transform in form or character.
• metamorphose = to undergo or cause a complete change in form
• From Greek: 'metamorphoun' — to transform
• 'Metamorphosis' = the change; figuratively: any dramatic transformation

quiz.question: The minister's grandiose promises of transformation had produced nothing. What does 'grandiose' mean?
options: Impressively large in scale but often empty or excessive
Too ambitious to be achieved with the available resources
Appealing in theory but impossible to deliver in practice
Significant enough to attract attention and build support
quiz.answer: Impressively large in scale but often empty or excessive
quiz.explanation:
Grandiose means impressively large — but usually excessively so, with more show than substance.
• grandiose = impressive or magnificent in appearance; but often foolishly ambitious
• From French: 'grandiose' — grand
• 'Grandiose scheme', 'grandiose claims' — bigger than reality can support

quiz.question: The lengthy disclaimer was otiose — nothing it said was legally significant. What does 'otiose' mean?
options: Serving no practical purpose — superfluous and useless
Too long to be read by anyone affected by it
Written more to protect the writer than to inform the reader
Included as a formality without adding any real value
quiz.answer: Serving no practical purpose — superfluous and useless
quiz.explanation:
Otiose means serving no useful purpose — redundant and idle.
• otiose = producing no useful result; unnecessary; idle
• From Latin: 'otiosus' — at leisure, idle
• 'An otiose clause', 'otiose effort' — wasted because it achieves nothing

quiz.question: The bank threatened to foreclose on the mortgage if payments were not resumed. What does 'foreclose' mean?
options: To take possession of a mortgaged property due to non-payment
To end a loan agreement and demand immediate repayment
To sell a mortgaged property to recover an outstanding debt, as the evidence showed
To pursue legal action against a borrower who has defaulted
quiz.answer: To take possession of a mortgaged property due to non-payment
quiz.explanation:
To foreclose means to repossess — when a lender takes back property because of mortgage default.
• foreclose = to take possession of a mortgaged property on default
• From Old French: 'forclos' — excluded
• 'Foreclosure' = the legal process; 'foreclose on' = take back the property

quiz.question: She was required to disclose any conflicts of interest before the vote. What does 'disclose' mean?
options: To make known or reveal information previously hidden
To declare a connection that might influence a decision
To publish information required by a transparency rule
To reveal something to those who have a right to know it
quiz.answer: To make known or reveal information previously hidden
quiz.explanation:
To disclose means to reveal — to make known what was hidden or undeclared.
• disclose = to make information known; to reveal
• From Latin: 'discludere' — to open up
• 'Disclosure', 'non-disclosure agreement' — revealing or withholding information

quiz.question: His lachrymose account of the misfortune had the audience reaching for handkerchiefs. What does 'lachrymose' mean?
options: Tearful or given to weeping — sad and sentimental
So moving it caused an emotional reaction in listeners
Dwelling on suffering in a way intended to produce sympathy
Delivered with such feeling that tears were a natural response
quiz.answer: Tearful or given to weeping — sad and sentimental
quiz.explanation:
Lachrymose means tearful — tending to weep or inducing sadness.
• lachrymose = mournful; given to tears
• From Latin: 'lacrima' — a tear
• 'A lachrymose tale' = one that produces tears; 'lachrymose mood' = weepy

quiz.question: The situation was tightening like a noose around the suspect's neck. What is a noose?
options: A loop with a running knot that tightens under pressure
A rope tied in a way that constricts when pulled
A type of knot historically used in hangings
A sliding loop that closes tighter as tension increases, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A loop with a running knot that tightens under pressure
quiz.explanation:
A noose is a loop with a sliding knot — it tightens as tension is applied.
• noose = a loop with a running knot that tightens when pulled
• From Latin: 'nodus' — a knot
• 'A noose around one's neck' = figuratively, a trap that tightens

quiz.question: The new regulation poses a significant challenge for small businesses. What does 'pose' mean here?
options: To present or constitute a problem or question
To create a difficulty that must be addressed
To create conditions in which businesses will struggle
To impose obligations that many businesses cannot meet
quiz.answer: To present or constitute a problem or question
quiz.explanation:
To pose means to present or constitute — 'pose a challenge' = constitute a challenge.
• pose = to put forward; to present (a problem, question, or threat)
• From French: 'poser' — to put, place
• 'Pose a threat', 'pose a question' — presenting something that demands a response

quiz.question: The exhibition juxtaposed ancient artefacts with modern interpretations of the same themes. What does 'juxtapose' mean?
options: To place side by side for comparison or contrast
To exhibit together two items with related themes
To draw a visual connection between old and new objects
To combine contrasting elements within a single display
quiz.answer: To place side by side for comparison or contrast
quiz.explanation:
To juxtapose means to place side by side — to highlight contrast or connection.
• juxtapose = to place next to each other for comparison or contrast
• From Latin: 'juxta' (next to) + French 'poser' (to place)
• 'Juxtaposed images' = placed beside each other to invite comparison

quiz.question: The military deposed the elected president in a midnight coup. What does 'depose' mean?
options: To remove from power — especially from a throne or office
To arrest and imprison a head of government by force
To end the rule of someone through a military intervention
To replace a leader by force before an election could be held
quiz.answer: To remove from power — especially from a throne or office
quiz.explanation:
To depose means to remove from power — to oust from a position of authority.
• depose = to remove from power; to dethrone
• From Latin: 'deponere' — to put down
• 'Deposed monarch', 'deposed in a coup' — removed from the seat of power

quiz.question: The government imposed new taxes on fuel. What does 'impose' mean?
options: To force something upon others — to put in place by authority
To introduce a measure that applies to all affected parties, as the evidence showed
To enact a law that creates a financial obligation
To require compliance with a new policy or regulation
quiz.answer: To force something upon others — to put in place by authority
quiz.explanation:
To impose means to put in place by authority — to enforce on others.
• impose = to force on others; to introduce with authority
• From Latin: 'imponere' — to put upon
• 'Impose sanctions', 'impose a curfew' — using authority to enforce

quiz.question: The director superimposed the name of the location over the first shot. What does 'superimpose' mean?
options: To place one image or element over another
To overlay text on a video image
To add text or graphics to footage in post-production
To layer one visual element on top of another in editing
quiz.answer: To place one image or element over another
quiz.explanation:
To superimpose means to place on top — to lay one thing over another.
• superimpose = to place or lay something over something else
• From Latin: 'super-' (over) + 'imponere' (to place)
• 'Superimposed text', 'superimposed images' — one placed directly over another

quiz.question: She composed herself before walking into the meeting. What does 'compose' mean here?
options: To calm oneself and restore emotional control
To prepare mentally for a difficult or important situation
To control a display of emotion in order to appear confident
To gather one's thoughts before entering a formal setting
quiz.answer: To calm oneself and restore emotional control
quiz.explanation:
To compose oneself means to regain calm — to restore emotional equilibrium.
• compose = to arrange; to calm oneself
• From Latin: 'componere' — to put together
• 'Composed' = calm; 'compose music' = create it

quiz.question: The leaves decomposed over winter and enriched the soil. What does 'decompose' mean?
options: To break down into simpler components through natural processes
To decay and return organic matter to the soil
To break down through the action of bacteria and weather, as the evidence showed
To rot and release nutrients back into the ground
quiz.answer: To break down into simpler components through natural processes
quiz.explanation:
To decompose means to break down — to decay into simpler substances.
• decompose = to break down through decay; to rot
• From Latin: 'de-' + 'componere' (to put together) — undoing composition
• 'Decomposing matter', 'decompose a problem' — breaking into parts

quiz.question: He interposed himself between the two quarreling colleagues. What does 'interpose' mean?
options: To place oneself between two parties or opposing forces
To physically stand between two people who are arguing
To intervene before a disagreement could become violent
To create a physical barrier to separate two individuals
quiz.answer: To place oneself between two parties or opposing forces
quiz.explanation:
To interpose means to place between — to put oneself or something in the middle.
• interpose = to insert between; to come between
• From Latin: 'interponere' — to place between
• 'Interpose a remark', 'interpose physically' — inserting oneself into a situation

quiz.question: The evidence was disposed of before investigators could examine it. What does 'dispose of' mean?
options: To get rid of something — to throw away or destroy
To hide evidence in a place unlikely to be searched
To move something away from where it could be found
To destroy something before it could become incriminating
quiz.answer: To get rid of something — to throw away or destroy
quiz.explanation:
To dispose of means to get rid of — to throw away, sell, or destroy.
• dispose of = to get rid of; to deal with
• From Latin: 'disponere' — to arrange
• 'Dispose of waste', 'dispose of assets' — removing from possession

quiz.question: The musician transposed the piece from C major to G major. What does 'transpose' mean?
options: To move or transfer something to a different position or key
To change the key of a piece to suit a different instrument, as the evidence showed
To rewrite music at a different pitch for a new performer
To alter the harmonic structure of a composition
quiz.answer: To move or transfer something to a different position or key
quiz.explanation:
To transpose means to transfer to a different position — or in music, to a different key.
• transpose = to cause to change positions; to write in a different key
• From Latin: 'transponere' — to put across
• 'Transpose two letters', 'transpose a melody' — moving to a different place or pitch

quiz.question: The novelist's prose was lyrical and precise — every sentence perfectly balanced. What is prose?
options: Written language in ordinary form — not verse
A style of writing that aspires to the qualities of poetry
The language a novelist uses as opposed to a poet
Descriptive or narrative writing distinguished from dialogue
quiz.answer: Written language in ordinary form — not verse
quiz.explanation:
Prose is ordinary written language — as opposed to poetry or verse.
• prose = written language that is not in verse
• From Latin: 'prosa oratio' — straightforward speech
• 'Prose style', 'write in prose' — the default form of most writing

quiz.question: The patient remained comatose for three days after the accident. What does 'comatose' mean?
options: In a state of deep unconsciousness — unable to be roused
Sedated and unresponsive following emergency treatment, as the evidence showed
Unconscious and requiring life support to survive
Too deeply asleep to respond to voice or touch
quiz.answer: In a state of deep unconsciousness — unable to be roused
quiz.explanation:
Comatose means in a coma — deeply unconscious and unable to be awakened.
• comatose = in a coma; profoundly unconscious
• From Greek: 'koma' — deep sleep
• Also colloquial: 'I was practically comatose by 10pm' = utterly exhausted

quiz.question: After six months of sobriety, he relapsed and returned to drinking. What does 'relapse' mean?
options: To fall back into a previous bad state after improvement
To return to an addiction after a period of abstinence
To abandon progress after a setback or difficult period, as the evidence showed
To regress following a sustained period of recovery
quiz.answer: To fall back into a previous bad state after improvement
quiz.explanation:
To relapse means to fall back — to return to a worse condition after recovery.
• relapse = to return to a previous worse state; to fall back into bad habits
• From Latin: 'relabi' — to slip back
• 'Relapse into depression', 'relapse rate' — the return to a worse condition

quiz.question: She caught a brief glimpse of the rare bird before it disappeared into the trees. What is a glimpse?
options: A very brief or partial view
A sighting that was too short to observe in detail
A momentary view before a subject moved from sight
The kind of fleeting observation that birdwatchers hope for
quiz.answer: A very brief or partial view
quiz.explanation:
A glimpse is a momentary, incomplete view — barely enough to register what was seen.
• glimpse = a brief or partial view; a quick look
• From Middle English: 'glimsen' — to shine faintly
• 'A glimpse of the future', 'catch a glimpse' — fleeting, partial perception

quiz.question: The cast rehearsed for six weeks before the opening night. What does 'rehearse' mean?
options: To practise a performance before the official presentation
To run through a play or piece repeatedly to prepare
To prepare for a public performance through repeated practice
To learn lines and actions in preparation for a live audience
quiz.answer: To practise a performance before the official presentation
quiz.explanation:
To rehearse means to practise — to prepare for a performance by repeated run-throughs.
• rehearse = to practise a play, speech, or other performance
• From Old French: 're-' + 'hercer' — to harrow again (going over again and again)
• 'Dress rehearsal' = final full practice; 'rehearse an argument' = prepare it

quiz.question: The coarse sand at the beach was uncomfortable to lie on. What does 'coarse' mean?
options: Rough and harsh in texture
Consisting of large particles compared to fine sand
Unpleasant because it lacked the smoothness of finer beaches
Abrasive enough to cause irritation to the skin
quiz.answer: Rough and harsh in texture
quiz.explanation:
Coarse means rough in texture — not fine or smooth.
• coarse = rough in texture; also crude in behavior or language
• Origin uncertain; perhaps from 'course' (ordinary)
• 'Coarse sand', 'coarse language' — both lack refinement

quiz.question: His reply was characteristically terse: 'No.' What does 'terse' mean?
options: Sparing of words — brief and slightly curt
So brief as to seem rude or uninterested
Too short to convey any meaningful information
Deliberately short to signal that the topic was closed
quiz.answer: Sparing of words — brief and slightly curt
quiz.explanation:
Terse means brief and to the point — often with a slightly curt edge.
• terse = neatly brief; using as few words as possible
• From Latin: 'tersus' — wiped clean, polished
• 'A terse reply' gets to the point without warmth or elaboration
